topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of stale coffee and something faintly floral hung in the air of our shared room, a familiar, suffocating blanket. I found the evidence tucked beneath my textbook—a page from my private journal, meticulously annotated in a script that was definitely not mine. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the ache in my molars. When I confronted Alex, the air immediately grew thick. "What are you doing?" I managed, my voice dangerously level, though the tremor in my hands betrayed the simmering anger beneath my skin. Alex just looked up, a practiced, infuriatingly placid expression settling on their face. "Just... reading," they murmured, their eyes flicking toward the page before meeting mine. The dismissal in their tone was a deliberate, sharp barb. I didn't say anything more; I just snatched the book back, the cover feeling slick and alien in my grip, and retreated to my desk, the sound of my own ragged breathing the only evidence of the internal storm raging within me.

[story 2]

I watched the light catch the crease where my journal had been, a small, damning detail in the otherwise messy landscape of our room. It wasn't just that Alex had been reading it; it was the casual way they’d turned the pages, as if they were inspecting a particularly dull piece of trash. A cold, hard knot formed in my stomach, tightening with every second of silence. "Seriously?" I asked, the words clipped and devoid of warmth. My gaze was fixed on their face, searching for any flicker of remorse, finding only a stubborn set to their mouth. They shrugged, a gesture so utterly unconcerned it made my skin crawl. "It was interesting," Alex replied, their tone suggesting that my innermost thoughts were no more compelling than a discarded wrapper. I felt a slow burn start low in my chest, a heat that demanded a reaction, but I held back, forcing my expression into a mask of bored indifference. It was far more satisfying to watch them squirm under the weight of their own poor judgment than to erupt into a shouting match.

[story 3]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow when I saw the smudged ink tracing my anxieties across the page. I stood there, breathing shallowly, the room suddenly too small, the air too thin. When Alex finally admitted to looking through my private thoughts, the sound that escaped me was a low, grating sound, more like gravel shifting than actual speech. "You have no right," I stated, the words tasting metallic on my tongue. I didn't raise my voice, which somehow made it infinitely more menacing. I walked over to the bed, deliberately placing my things down with exaggerated, precise movements. I looked at Alex, my eyes flat and utterly devoid of sympathy. "It’s pathetic," I said, leaning against the doorframe, crossing my arms over my chest. "To invade someone's space like that. It speaks volumes about your own emptiness." The silence that followed was heavy, weighted by my judgment, and I waited, daring them to offer anything but another flimsy excuse.
